worth_it_explanation: Integral Ad Science (IAS) is MRC-accredited for both GIVT and SIVT detection across display, video, and CTV environments. These checks confirm that IAS measurement coverage is actually turned on across all active spend, that GIVT and SIVT are being reported separately so the team can distinguish routine bot traffic from fraud requiring active response, and that elevated findings trigger supply exclusions or refund pursuit rather than sitting unacknowledged in the dashboard. The work is primarily configuration review inside IAS Signal and the connected DSP, not a rebuild.
intro: IAS is a third-party ad verification vendor whose invalid traffic (IVT) detection is accredited by the Media Rating Council (MRC). IVT breaks into two categories defined by the MRC: general invalid traffic (GIVT), which is identified through routine means such as known bot lists and data center signatures, and sophisticated invalid traffic (SIVT), which mimics real user behavior and requires advanced analytics and multipoint corroboration to detect. IAS is accredited for both categories, including SIVT filtration in connected TV (CTV) environments. These checks confirm that IAS coverage is active across all active media, that the IAS Signal reporting platform separates GIVT from SIVT for actionable triage, and that teams follow through when IAS flags elevated fraud exposure.

audit_checks.title: IAS invalid traffic measurement is not active across all active media
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Spend on any line item, campaign, or channel where IAS measurement is not enabled produces no IVT signal. Without measurement coverage, the team has no basis for identifying fraud on that portion of the buy, no data to support a supply-quality conversation with the DSP, and no record of IVT rates if a refund or make-good is needed.

educate.base:
Integral Ad Science (IAS) measures invalid traffic (IVT) by integrating with the demand-side platform (DSP) or ad server through a tag or server-to-server connection. For measurement to produce data, that connection must be active on the specific line items or campaigns that are running. A DSP account with an IAS integration does not automatically extend that measurement to every line item. Each line item or insertion order must be configured to pass impression data to IAS, or the IAS tag must be wrapped around the creative.

The Media Rating Council (MRC) defines IVT as traffic generated by bots or any form of nonhuman activity that should not count in measurement. IVT breaks into two categories. General invalid traffic (GIVT) is identified through routine means: data center IP lists, known crawler signatures, and the IAB Tech Lab Spiders and Bots list. Sophisticated invalid traffic (SIVT) is harder to detect because it mimics real user behavior through mechanisms such as ad stacking, pixel stuffing, and bot networks that operate within real browser environments. IAS is MRC-accredited for both categories.

Coverage gaps are easy to overlook when a campaign launches under time pressure or when new line items are added to an existing flight. The result is that a portion of the total buy runs without any IVT measurement, and any fraud occurring on that portion goes undetected for the duration of the flight.

A few signs point to incomplete IAS measurement coverage:
  • The IAS Signal dashboard shows a campaign count or impression total lower than what the DSP reports for the same date range and advertiser
  • Specific line items in the DSP show delivery but do not appear in the IAS Signal campaign view
  • Programmatic video or connected TV (CTV) line items are running but no CTV IVT data appears in IAS Signal for the same period
You may want to compare the active campaign and line item count in the DSP directly against the campaigns visible in IAS Signal before concluding that coverage is complete.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retail advertiser, any line item driving traffic to product pages or shopping feeds that lacks IAS measurement coverage is also missing a fraud baseline for that portion of the funnel. You may want to prioritize confirming IAS coverage on the highest-spend open exchange and programmatic video line items, as those formats carry the most IVT exposure and represent the largest potential refund opportunity if fraud is later identified.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, an uncovered line item that shows low conversion rates may be generating bot traffic that inflates delivery without producing form submissions. You may want to confirm that IAS measurement is active on any display or video line item where the conversion rate is unexpectedly low relative to other placements with similar targeting.

learn_more.source_title: Media Rating Council: Invalid Traffic Detection and Filtration Standards
What is the IAB Tech Lab Spiders and Bots list?

educate.learn_more_note: IAS Signal campaign setup documentation and integration guides require an IAS partner login to access. Contact your IAS account team or log in at signal.integralads.com to review coverage configuration for your account.

investigate.intro: Compare the campaigns and line items actively running in the DSP against those appearing in IAS Signal to identify any gaps in IVT measurement coverage.

ias_signal.label: IAS Signal
steps: Log in to IAS Signal and navigate to the campaign view. Set the date range to match the current active flight period.
Note the total number of campaigns and the total impression count shown in IAS Signal for the advertiser.
In the DSP, navigate to the campaign or line item view for the same advertiser and date range. Record the total number of active line items and the total impression count.

Compare the two counts. If the IAS Signal impression total is meaningfully lower than the DSP total, or if specific line item names visible in the DSP do not appear in IAS Signal, those line items are not passing IVT data to IAS.

For any gap identified, check the DSP line item settings to confirm whether the IAS integration tag is applied to the creative or whether the server-to-server integration is configured for that line item. Record which line items are missing coverage.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the total impression count in IAS Signal and the total impression count in the DSP for the same advertiser and date range. A discrepancy of more than 5% between the two counts, or any specific line item visible in the DSP that does not appear in IAS Signal, is a fail recorded with the line item name, its format type, and the approximate impression volume running without IAS coverage.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the IAS Signal impression total, the DSP impression total, and any line items missing from IAS Signal above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s IAS invalid traffic (IVT) measurement is not active across all active media. Specific line items are delivering impressions without passing data to IAS Signal, leaving that portion of the buy with no IVT measurement and no basis for fraud-related refund or make-good requests. The Media Rating Council (MRC) distinguishes between general invalid traffic (GIVT), identified through routine means such as known bot lists, and sophisticated invalid traffic (SIVT), which requires advanced detection. Without IAS coverage on all line items, neither category can be identified for the uncovered spend. {{voice_recommend}} auditing the active line item list in the DSP against the campaigns visible in IAS Signal, enabling the IAS integration on any line items currently missing coverage, and confirming that connected TV (CTV) and programmatic video line items are included, as those formats benefit from IAS's MRC-accredited SIVT filtration.

audit_checks.title: IAS IVT reporting does not separate GIVT from SIVT, preventing triage
audit_checks.impact_rationale: GIVT and SIVT require different responses. GIVT is routine bot activity that filtration addresses automatically; an elevated GIVT rate signals a supply quality problem worth addressing through exclusions. SIVT is fraud that mimics real users and may indicate organized ad fraud on a specific placement. Reviewing a single combined IVT rate without the split obscures which problem is present and prevents the team from prioritizing correctly.

educate.base:
The MRC distinguishes invalid traffic (IVT) into two categories with distinct detection methods and practical implications. General invalid traffic (GIVT) is identified through routine means: IP lists, known crawler signatures, and the IAB Tech Lab Spiders and Bots list, which catalogs user agents associated with automated traffic. GIVT represents a baseline level of non-human activity that filtration handles through standardized processes. Sophisticated invalid traffic (SIVT) requires advanced analytics, behavioral analysis, and multipoint corroboration to detect because it is designed to look like legitimate human activity. SIVT encompasses ad stacking, pixel stuffing, hijacked devices, and bot networks that cycle through real browser environments to avoid standard detection.

IAS is MRC-accredited for both categories, including SIVT filtration in connected TV (CTV) environments. The IAS Signal platform can surface GIVT and SIVT as separate line items in its reporting views. When those categories are collapsed into a single IVT percentage, the team loses the ability to distinguish a routine data center traffic issue from a deliberate fraud scheme targeting a specific placement or supply source.

Reviewing the GIVT and SIVT breakdown also matters for refund conversations. DSPs and supply-side platforms (SSPs) typically respond differently to GIVT findings versus SIVT findings. SIVT at scale on a specific placement is a stronger basis for requesting a make-good than a blended IVT rate that may reflect mostly routine bot filtering.

A few signs point to GIVT and SIVT not being reviewed separately:
  • The team references a single blended IVT rate when discussing fraud exposure without mentioning the composition
  • IAS Signal is accessed primarily through a high-level summary view rather than a filtered breakdown by IVT type
  • Reporting shared internally or with clients shows a combined IVT metric without the GIVT and SIVT split
You may want to locate the GIVT versus SIVT breakdown in IAS Signal before concluding that the reported IVT rate is well understood.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retail advertiser, SIVT on product-page-driving line items is a stronger signal of deliberate fraud than GIVT from the same placements. You may want to check whether any line items with high blended IVT rates also show elevated SIVT specifically. That combination points to a supply source that warrants exclusion rather than a routine filtration issue.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, SIVT on display or video line items that show low conversion rates is particularly worth isolating. If a line item's SIVT rate is elevated while its GIVT rate is normal, the fraud pattern is more likely to be deliberately targeting that placement, and the response should include supply exclusions and a refund request rather than passive filtration.

learn_more.source_title: Media Rating Council: Invalid Traffic Detection and Filtration Standards
What is the IAB Tech Lab Spiders and Bots list?

educate.learn_more_note: IAS Signal reporting views and GIVT versus SIVT breakdown filters require an IAS partner login. Contact your IAS account team or log in at signal.integralads.com to access the full IVT type breakdown for your account.

investigate.intro: Locate the GIVT and SIVT breakdown in IAS Signal and confirm that reporting shared with the team includes both categories separately.

ias_signal.label: IAS Signal
steps: Log in to IAS Signal and navigate to the fraud or IVT reporting section. Set the date range to the last 30 days.

Look for a reporting view or filter that separates general invalid traffic (GIVT) from sophisticated invalid traffic (SIVT). IAS Signal surfaces these as distinct IVT type categories in its fraud reporting.
Record the GIVT rate and the SIVT rate separately for the advertiser and for the highest-spend line items. Note whether any line item shows a SIVT rate above the account average.

Check whether any reports shared with the client or internal team display the GIVT and SIVT split or only a combined IVT total. If only the combined figure is shared, note that the breakdown is available in IAS Signal and should be included in reporting.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner confirms whether IAS Signal displays GIVT and SIVT as separate metrics in the fraud reporting view for the active campaign set. If the GIVT and SIVT breakdown is available and is being reviewed as part of regular fraud monitoring, the check passes. If the team is reviewing only a combined IVT metric without the split, or if the split is available in IAS Signal but has not been used to triage specific placements showing elevated SIVT, the check is a fail recorded with the blended IVT rate and a note on whether SIVT data is present but unreviewed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the GIVT rate and SIVT rate from IAS Signal for the last 30 days above, along with any placements showing elevated SIVT specifically, to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s IAS invalid traffic (IVT) reporting is being reviewed as a combined metric without separating general invalid traffic (GIVT) from sophisticated invalid traffic (SIVT). The MRC defines GIVT as traffic identified through routine filtration means and SIVT as fraud that mimics real user behavior and requires advanced analytics to detect. IAS is MRC-accredited for both categories. Without reviewing the GIVT and SIVT split, the team cannot distinguish a routine data center traffic issue from a deliberate fraud scheme targeting a specific placement, and cannot prioritize supply exclusions or refund requests appropriately. {{voice_recommend}} accessing the GIVT versus SIVT breakdown in IAS Signal, flagging any line items with elevated SIVT rates for supply exclusions, and including the split in all internal and client-facing fraud reporting going forward.

audit_checks.title: Elevated IVT findings from IAS are not acted on with supply exclusions or refund requests
audit_checks.impact_rationale: IVT measurement that does not trigger a response leaves the account continuing to buy from supply sources IAS has already identified as fraudulent. The same budget continues reaching non-human traffic on the same placements until a human reviews the IAS Signal data and acts on it. Without a defined threshold for action and a process for requesting refunds or applying exclusions, the measurement produces no downstream improvement in spend quality.

educate.base:
IAS produces IVT data through MRC-accredited detection covering both general invalid traffic (GIVT) and sophisticated invalid traffic (SIVT). The data is only useful if it informs action. Two specific actions address elevated IVT findings: supply exclusions applied in the DSP to prevent future buys from the offending placement, and refund or make-good requests submitted to the DSP or supply source for IVT that has already delivered.

DSPs typically have a defined IVT credit process. The advertiser or agency identifies placements where IAS Signal shows IVT above the agreed contractual threshold, documents the finding with the IAS Signal report, and submits a request to the DSP or publisher. The DSP reviews the claim against its own measurement records. Where the parties agree on the IVT rate, a credit or make-good impression block is issued. The TAG Certified Against Fraud program establishes industry guidelines that certified companies follow in handling these claims, and IAS's TAG certification status provides context for the evidentiary standard the claim needs to meet.

Supply exclusions work in the other direction: rather than recovering spend already lost, they prevent future spend from reaching the same placements. The exclusion is applied at the line item or advertiser level in the DSP, using the domain or app bundle identifier from the IAS Signal placement report.

A few signs point to elevated IVT findings not being acted on:
  • IAS Signal shows one or more placements with IVT rates above 5% that have not been added to the DSP exclusion list
  • No refund or make-good request has been submitted to the DSP despite IAS Signal showing IVT at or above the contractual threshold on a completed flight
  • The team treats IAS Signal as a reporting tool only and has no defined process for translating findings into exclusions or claims
You may want to review the IAS Signal placement-level IVT report alongside the current DSP exclusion list before concluding that findings are being acted on.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retail advertiser, a placement showing elevated IVT that also appears in the DSP's delivery report for product-page traffic is consuming budget that should reach real shoppers. You may want to confirm that any such placement has been excluded from active line items and that the spend against it during the affected period has been documented for a make-good request.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, a placement with elevated SIVT rates on lead-gen line items warrants both an exclusion and a refund request. The inflated impressions represent budget that produced no legitimate audience exposure. You may want to cross-reference the IAS Signal SIVT findings with the DSP's spend-by-placement breakdown to calculate the portion of spend eligible for a make-good.

learn_more.source_title: Media Rating Council: Invalid Traffic Detection and Filtration Standards

educate.learn_more_note: IAS Signal's placement-level IVT reporting and the process for exporting findings to support refund claims require an IAS partner login. Contact your IAS account team or log in at signal.integralads.com to access the placement-level fraud exposure report.

investigate.intro: Review the IAS Signal placement-level IVT report and confirm whether elevated findings have been translated into supply exclusions in the DSP or documented for refund requests.

ias_signal.label: IAS Signal
steps: Log in to IAS Signal and navigate to the fraud or IVT reporting section. Set the date range to cover the last completed flight period, at a minimum the last 30 days.
Filter the report to show placement-level (domain or app bundle) IVT rates. Sort by IVT rate descending to identify the placements with the highest fraud exposure.

Note any placement where the IVT rate exceeds 5% or exceeds the contractual IVT threshold agreed with the DSP or publisher. Record the placement name, IVT rate, GIVT rate, SIVT rate, and impression volume for each flagged placement.

In the DSP, open the advertiser-level or line-item-level exclusion list and check whether the flagged placements from the previous step are present. If a placement with elevated IVT is not on the exclusion list, record it as an unresolved finding.

Check whether any refund or make-good request has been submitted to the DSP for the flagged placements. If no request has been initiated, document the IVT rate, the impression volume, and the spend value against that placement from the DSP reporting tool as the basis for a claim.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each placement in IAS Signal with an IVT rate above 5% (or above the contractual threshold). For each such placement, the practitioner confirms whether it has been added to the DSP exclusion list and whether a refund or make-good request has been submitted or documented. A placement that has been excluded from active buying and has had a refund request initiated passes. A placement with elevated IVT that remains in the active buy pool with no exclusion and no refund documentation is a fail, recorded with the placement name, IVT rate, and estimated spend affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the IAS Signal placement-level IVT rates and the current DSP exclusion list entries above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s elevated IVT findings from IAS are not being translated into supply exclusions or refund requests. IAS Signal shows one or more placements with IVT rates above threshold, including sophisticated invalid traffic (SIVT) that IAS detects through MRC-accredited behavioral analysis, but those placements remain in the active buy pool. The same supply sources continue receiving budget on each subsequent flight. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the IAS Signal placement-level IVT report for the last 30 days, adding any placement with an IVT rate above the contractual threshold to the DSP exclusion list, and documenting the impression volume and spend against each elevated-IVT placement as the basis for a make-good request to the DSP. {{voice_recommend}} establishing a defined IVT review cadence, at a minimum monthly, so elevated findings are identified and acted on within a reasonable window rather than accumulating across flights.
